reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: All Stacktape config examples correctly use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> with the exact label 'TypeScript' — no forbidden YAML blocks, no fenced TypeScript defineConfig blocks, no StacktapeConfig type style.

The config-time vs CloudFormation-time classification table in 'Resolution phases' is the highest-signal content on the page. It gives a reader an immediate mental model for the two-phase system without needing to read every directive section.
CLI command examples are correctly split into separate fenced bash blocks with prose labels above each — the copy-button trap is fully avoided.

Strong FAQ with genuine mix of Stacktape-specific, underlying-AWS-behavior, and comparison questions. The 'Should I use directives or TypeScript conditionals?' entry is opinionated and actionable — exactly what this audience needs.
Decision support throughout: every major section tells the reader *when* to use the feature and what the tradeoff is, not just how to configure it.
Realistic, copyable code examples in every section. The 'Callback parameters' example branching on `isProduction` is a real-world pattern readers can drop into their own configs.
The Tip callout for $CliArgs reading standard CLI args is well-placed and adds genuine value without padding.
problems: The claim that embedded directives like `api-$Stage()-endpoint` are 'automatically converted to the equivalent $Format(...)' is not grounded in the provided source file (built-in-directives.ts only defines the directives; the auto-detection logic lives elsewhere in the resolver). The claim may be accurate but reads as asserted rather than sourced.

The $GitInfo properties table (branch, commit, username, gitUrl, hasUncommitedChanges) cannot be verified from the provided source — gitInfoManager.gitInfo is not in the source files. The error path in the source says 'Valid arguments are:' followed by Object.keys(gitInfo), but the actual keys are not listed.

The defineConfig callback parameters table (especially the `user` object with `id`, `name`, `email`) is not grounded in the provided source. The source file covers directives only, not the callback signature.

The $CfStackOutput 'picks up the new value on its next update' claim is an oversimplification. CloudFormation stacks linked via Fn::ImportValue (which $CfStackOutput produces) have restricted update semantics — the exporting stack cannot delete or modify an export while another stack is consuming it via ImportValue. This can surprise users who expect a clean live-link.

$Secret is described as a 'CloudFormation-time directive' that 'produces CloudFormation intrinsic functions'. The source shows it actually produces a CloudFormation *dynamic reference* (`{{resolve:secretsmanager:...}}`), not a CF intrinsic function. The distinction matters because dynamic references and intrinsic functions have different constraints (dynamic references can't be used in all CF resource properties). The page's framing bundles them together, which could mislead someone hitting a 'dynamic reference not supported here' error.

No <ApiReference> on the page. For a deployment/concept page this may be intentional, but if there is a type definition covering directive syntax or defineConfig callback shape it would add value at the bottom.
optionalImprovements: Ground the embedded directive auto-conversion claim or soften the wording: 'Stacktape detects directives embedded in string values and resolves them inline' — then cite the directive reference page for exact behavior rather than asserting the $Format equivalence, which isn't in the provided source.

Add a caveat to the $CfStackOutput section: 'CloudFormation prevents updating or deleting an export while any stack is consuming it via ImportValue. This means $CfStackOutput creates a hard dependency — deleting or renaming the exporting stack's output requires first removing all consumers.' This is the main gotcha for cross-stack references that bites real teams.

For $Secret, distinguish 'dynamic references' from 'intrinsic functions': 'The directive produces a CloudFormation dynamic reference (`{{resolve:secretsmanager:...}}`), which CloudFormation resolves at stack creation time. Note that dynamic references cannot be used in all resource properties — if you get a "dynamic references are not supported" error, use $SsmParam or inject the value through a Custom Resource instead.'

The $GitInfo section lists five properties but qualifies none as 'only available if git history is present'. Add a single sentence: 'Properties like commit and gitUrl may be unavailable in shallow clones or detached HEAD states; Stacktape will fall back to CI environment variables automatically.' This prevents a CI debugging surprise.

The last FAQ entry about `stacktape dev` behavior for CloudFormation-time directives is detailed and useful but is based on logic inferred from the source's `localResolveFunction` branches. Consider framing it as 'during dev mode, $Secret fetches the actual secret value from Secrets Manager...' with a link to the dev mode docs rather than describing the disableEmulation flag in the FAQ — the flag behavior is accurate per source but is an advanced escape hatch that may distract beginners.

Consider adding a short intro note that YAML configs support all directives too (since the source doesn't restrict them to TypeScript). Currently the page's first paragraph mentions TypeScript configs for the callback parameters, which is correct, but a reader might infer directives are TypeScript-only.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Excellent separation of config-time vs CloudFormation-time directives — the 'Resolution phases' table is the clearest mental model for this distinction and readers can act on it immediately.
Comprehensive FAQ covering Stacktape-specific, underlying AWS, and comparison questions — well above the minimum bar, each answer is 2-4 sentences and self-contained.
Shell command examples correctly split into separate fenced blocks with prose labels (no copy-button trap).
All Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}/>; no YAML Stacktape configs, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TypeScript for Stacktape config.
The `$CfStackOutput` vs `$StackOutput` tradeoff (snapshot vs live ImportValue) is explained accurately and concisely — this is the question most teams hit in production.
The CI fallback env-var details for `$GitInfo()` (GitHub Actions, GitLab, Bitbucket) are a practical production gotcha that most docs skip.
problems: $Var() and $This() are listed in the 'Resolution phases' config-time directive enumeration but never documented anywhere on the page. A reader who sees them in that list will search for an explanation and find nothing. Either add a brief paragraph for each or remove them from the enumeration.

The embedded directive syntax claim ('api-$Stage()-endpoint is automatically converted to the equivalent $Format(...)') is not grounded in the provided source file (built-in-directives.ts does not implement string interpolation scanning). If this is supported, cite it; if it's handled by the config resolver layer, note that the source is in the config resolver rather than built-in-directives.ts.

The defineConfig callback parameters table lists projectName, command, awsProfile, cliArgs, and user — none of these are verifiable from the provided source file. The source only confirms stage (globalStateManager.targetStack.stage) and region (globalStateManager.region). Including ungrounded fields in a reference table risks misleading readers about what's actually available.

$CfStackOutput behavior is slightly misrepresented: the source shows that its resolveFunction calls the same resolveStackOutput helper as $StackOutput and validates the stack exists via awsSdkManager.getStackDetails at config time before emitting the ImportValue. The page implies it is purely a CF-time resolution with no config-time activity, which is inaccurate.
optionalImprovements: Add a brief 'Variables and config reuse' subsection for $Var() (returns the config's variables block) and $This() (returns the entire raw config) — two sentences each is enough; the current page lists them in the resolution phases but leaves readers with no explanation.

Add a caveat or source-grounded note to the embedded directive auto-conversion claim before shipping; if unverifiable from the provided source, remove or qualify it ('when a directive appears inside a string property, Stacktape detects and converts it — see the configuration reference for full details').

Tighten the defineConfig callback parameters table to only the fields confirmed by the directive source (stage, region, awsProfile/profile, awsAccountId); for the others, link to the TypeScript type definition or add a 'see the TypeScript type for the full list' note rather than enumerating possibly-wrong fields.

Note that $CfStackOutput still validates the referenced stack and output exist at config time (it calls the same AWS SDK stack lookup as $StackOutput); the only runtime difference is that the embedded value is a CloudFormation Fn::ImportValue rather than a static string.

The $SsmParam section could note that Stacktape pins the specific parameter version ID in the CloudFormation dynamic reference ({{resolve:ssm:...:VERSION}}), so the deployed stack always uses the version that existed at deploy time — relevant for teams rotating SSM parameters.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and clear about why deploy-time parameters matter.
Most H2 headings are compact and scannable, and the page uses tables effectively for directive and metadata summaries.
Stacktape config examples use the required `CodeBlock intellisense` TypeScript form rather than forbidden YAML or fenced TypeScript config.
The `$CliArgs()` section is practical: it explains the `--` separator, string/boolean capture behavior, defaults, and includes one command per shell block.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source file, especially the `defineConfig` callback parameter table (`projectName`, `command`, `awsProfile`, `cliArgs`, `user`) and the statement that directives work identically in YAML and TypeScript configs.

The page describes directive embedding as automatically converted to `$Format(...)`, but the provided `built-in-directives.ts` source does not show that conversion behavior. If this comes from another resolver, that source needs to be included; otherwise narrow or remove the claim.

The `$GitInfo()` CI fallback details are more specific than the provided source supports. The source only shows `$GitInfo` delegates to `gitInfoManager.gitInfo` and validates property names against that object.

The FAQ answer for local development makes specific claims about `stacktape dev`, deployed stack outputs, AWS APIs, and `--disableEmulation` behavior. Parts are partially grounded by `localResolveFunction`, but the broader dev-mode behavior is not fully supported by the supplied source.

The page omits a clear API/reference-style summary of all built-in directives from the source, including `$Var`, `$This`, `$ResourceParam`, and `$CfResourceParam`, even though it mentions some only in passing.

The FAQ is broad and useful, but it is mostly Stacktape-specific. It has limited choosing/comparison coverage and little underlying AWS-service context beyond CloudFormation, Secrets Manager, and SSM.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground the `defineConfig` callback parameter table. If only `built-in-directives.ts` is available, restrict the page to directive behavior proven by that file instead of documenting callback metadata.

Narrow unsupported conversion wording around embedded directives. Either cite/include the resolver source that proves automatic `$Format(...)` conversion, or state only the directive syntax proven by the provided source.

Revise `$GitInfo()` CI fallback claims unless the `git-info-manager` source is provided. Keep the grounded facts: `$GitInfo(property)` reads a property from git info and throws an error listing valid properties when the property is invalid.

Revise the dev-mode FAQ answer to match the source exactly: `localResolveFunction` exists for runtime directives, `$ResourceParam` can return `IDENTIFIER_FOR_MISSING_OUTPUT` when `disableEmulation` is set, and `$Secret()` / `$SsmParam()` fetch values through AWS SDK helpers. Avoid broader dev-mode claims not shown in source.
optionalImprovements: Add a compact directive matrix with columns for directive, phase (`isRuntime` true/false), required parameters, and main use case. This would make the page more extractable and complete.
Add short coverage for `$Var()` and `$This()` or explicitly link them to the directives reference if the page is intentionally focused on deploy-time values.

Add a brief “When not to use deploy-time parameters” section: use static config for values that should not vary by deploy, use secrets for sensitive values, and avoid CLI args for settings that need auditability.

Strengthen the FAQ mix with one or two comparison questions, such as “Should I use `$Secret()` or `$CliArgs()` for credentials?” and “Should cross-stack values use `$StackOutput()` or `$CfStackOutput()`?”

verifierResults.summary: The draft is mostly aligned with the provided directive implementation for directive names, runtime/config-time split, file loading, secret/SSM behavior, and stack output validation. The main publication risks are unsupported claims about TypeScript callback parameters, CLI parsing rules, GitInfo property names/fallbacks, and dev-mode behavior that are not grounded in the single provided source file.

issues.statement: “During stacktape dev, CloudFormation-time directives like $Secret() and $ResourceParam() resolve from the deployed stack's outputs and AWS APIs… If the referenced resource hasn't been deployed yet, the directive throws an error unless emulation is disabled with the --disableEmulation flag.”
issues.reason: The source supports local resolution paths for individual directives, but the draft overgeneralizes them. $ResourceParam local resolution reads deployed stack overview resources, while $Secret local resolution calls Secrets Manager and has the missing-version guard commented out. Say $ResourceParam local resolution reads the deployed stack overview and can return a missing-output identifier when --disableEmulation is set. Say $Secret local resolution fetches the secret value from Secrets Manager, and avoid claiming the same undeployed-resource behavior for it.

issues.statement: During `stacktape dev`, CloudFormation-time directives like `$Secret()` and `$ResourceParam()` resolve from the deployed stack's outputs and AWS APIs. `$Secret()` fetches the actual secret value from Secrets Manager. `$ResourceParam()` reads from the deployed stack overview. issues.reason: The `$ResourceParam()` local resolver supports the missing-output behavior described, but the `$Secret()` local resolver does not use `disableEmulation`; the disable-emulation branch is commented out. The blanket statement about CloudFormation-time directives is therefore wrong for `$Secret()`.
issues.suggestedFix: Split the local-dev behavior by directive. State that `$ResourceParam()` checks the deployed stack overview and may return the missing-output identifier when `--disableEmulation` is set. issues.statement: `$Secret()` actual value never appears in the CloudFormation template in plaintext.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and within the target length.
Configuration examples follow the required TypeScript class-based `<CodeBlock intellisense>` pattern and avoid forbidden YAML or `StacktapeConfig` examples.
The page is practical: it covers the major directive families, shows realistic Lambda environment-variable examples, and splits shell commands into separate copyable blocks.
FAQ is broad enough for this page, with useful questions about CLI args, formatting, secrets, cross-stack references, local development, and stage-specific config.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

The `$CliArgs()`, `$File()`, `$FileRaw()`, `$Stage()`, `$Region()`, `$Profile()`, `$AwsAccountId()`, `$Format()`, `$Var()`, `$This()`, `$ResourceParam()`, `$CfResourceParam()`, `$Secret()`, `$SsmParam()`, `$CfFormat()`, `$StackOutput()`, and `$CfStackOutput()` descriptions are mostly aligned with the provided implementation.
All markdown cross-links in the draft point to routes present in the supplied documentation navigation index.
